figures.caption: Fig. 1: The concept of mobile manipulation for explosive ordnance disposal in rescue scenarios.

Fig. 13: The typical robot performance during the tasks: (a-b) the end-effector states in grasping during locomotion, captured either 0.1s before gripper closure or at the moment when the end-effector is closest to the grasping pose; (c) the end-effector states near the objects during the object inspection task on rugged terrain.
Fig. 11: Raigor: a self-developed tracked mobile manipulator.
Fig. 3: The typical mechanism of a tracked mobile manipulator and its key reference frame and state definition.
Fig. 17: The typical end-effector linear velocity and acceleration patterns in the ablation experiment.
Fig. 8: The typical performance of different algorithms in grasping near obstacles.

Fig. 7: Four typical simulated rescue scenarios with distinct end-effector operation requirements: (a) obstacle-aware grasping with configuration adjustment; (b) grasping during locomotion; (c) environment inspection during locomotion; and (d) transportation of a force-sensitive payload over rough terrain.
Fig. 4: The typical arm span schematic description of a tracked mobile manipulator.
Fig. 15: The typical end-effector and payload motion traces in the ablation experiment (15s in total).
Fig. 14: The typical end-effector executed trajectories during the experiments. The coordinate frames in (a) and (b) indicate the optimal grasping poses.
Fig. 10: The typical executed trajectories of the end-effector in grasping during locomotion.
